text.title: Two-layer enforcement
text.summary: Each gate is enforced both at human-review time (readiness.md) and at machine-run time (CI). Both layers are required; one without the other lets bugs through.

rule.statement: readiness.md MUST contain a tick (with evidence link) for every required gate from the matrix, OR a "n/a + one-line reason" when the matrix says the gate does not apply.
rule.rationale: The human-review layer catches "the tests passed but the assertion is empty" failures that CI cannot see.

rule.statement: CI MUST actually RUN every required gate and fail the build on a non-pass result. A green CI is mandatory before the feature moves to done/.
rule.rationale: The CI layer catches "the developer skipped the test locally" failures that human review cannot see at speed.

section.title: Why both
section.p: Human-only enforcement → tests get marked OK on faith. CI-only enforcement → required tests get deleted "because they were flaky" and CI is none the wiser. Both layers together produce a system that fails closed when either single layer is gamed.

antipattern.description: Trusting CI alone and treating readiness.md item 4 as a rubber stamp.
antipattern.reason: The author of a flaky test usually marks the test as skipped or deletes it; CI then turns green; readiness.md still claims tests pass.

antipattern.description: Ticking readiness.md items without checking CI status.
antipattern.reason: readiness becomes paperwork. The CI run is the only thing that proves the test executed.
